did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning architectures, specifically a hybrid model combining VGG19 and ResNet50, along with transfer learning and data augmentation, to classify 7 types of skin cancer lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of hybrid CNN models (VGG19+ResNet50, etc.) on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology (data augmentation, transfer learning), results (87% overall F1 score), and new findings, despite being published in a conference proceedings (AISTS).

title: Leveraging Complementary CNNs with Hybrid Model Transfer Learning for Robust Multi-Class Skin Cancer Diagnosis
journal: 2025 Artificial Intelligence and Smart Technologies for Sustainability Conference (AISTS)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of multi-class skin cancer diagnosis by evaluating individual and hybrid deep learning models on the highly imbalanced HAM10000 dataset containing 10,005 dermoscopic images. The researchers employed data augmentation techniques, such as rotation and flipping, to balance the classes and utilized transfer learning with architectures including ResNet50, DenseNet121, and VGG19. A hybrid architecture combining VGG19 and ResNet50 was developed to leverage the complementary strengths of both networks for improved lesion classification. The model was trained and validated using an 80-20 split, with specific layers unfrozen to adapt pretrained weights to the skin cancer task. The key result demonstrated that the VGG19+ResNet50 hybrid model achieved an overall F1 score of 87% and classwise F1-scores ranging from 77% to 100%. These findings highlight the efficiency of hybrid CNN architectures in handling class imbalance and accurately classifying seven types of skin cancer lesions.

all_task_types.target: 7 types of skin cancer lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into 7 discrete diagnostic categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts specific lesion classes rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using F1-scores (87% overall) and accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The Human Against Machine 10000 dataset, a prominent and highly trusted dataset for skin cancer research containing dermatoscopic images. Ground truth was provided by histopathology for more than 50% of lesions, with the rest confirmed by follow-up examinations or in vivo confocal microscopy (IVCM).
classes_used: akiec (Actinic Keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease)

use.training: Used for training individual models (ResNet50, DenseNet121, VGG19) and hybrid models (VGG19+ResNet50, VGG19+DenseNet121) after data augmentation to balance classes.
use.validation: Used for validation with an 80-20 split of the dataset.
use.other: A subset of images (at least 50 or 20% from original data) was taken for testing purposes, but specific counts for the test set were not explicitly reported as a separate final metric in the results section.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assign additional weights to classes and handle class imbalance in the dataset.
To analyze true vs. predicted label ratios and class-wise performance for individual and hybrid models.
To evaluate and compare the performance of individual and hybrid models, specifically reporting an overall F1 score of 87% (or 86% in results section) and class-wise scores ranging from 77% to 100%.
To compare model performance, reporting values such as 87% for DenseNet121 and 88% for ResNet50/Weighted DenseNet121.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests were reported in the paper. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy and F1-scores.

HAM10000.evaluation_setting: Multi-class classification using individual and hybrid CNN models with transfer learning and data augmentation.

Augmented Data Hybrid Model (VGG19+ResNe.notes: Achieved classwise F1-scores ranging from 0.77 to 1.00. This model utilized data augmentation to handle class imbalance, resulting in the best overall performance.

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports that without handling data imbalance, classwise accuracy was poor. The application of SMOTE improved overall accuracy to 88% for Weighted DenseNet121 and ResNet50 but did not fully resolve classwise imbalance issues. The final best results were achieved using data augmentation combined with the VGG19+ResNet hybrid model, yielding an overall F1 score of 86% (or 87% in abstract) and classwise F1-scores between 0.77 and 1.00.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid CNN model combining VGG19 and ResNet50 (VGG19+ResNet50). Individual models evaluated included ResNet50, DenseNet121, and VGG19.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning approach using pretrained weights for both ResNet50 and VGG19.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (standard for HAM10000 dermoscopic images, but specific dimensions like 224x224 not stated).

architecture_details.hybrid_structure: Input layers split to ResNet50 and VGG19 blocks. After training blocks, a concatenation block merges the outputs. Followed by Dense1, Dropout, Dense2 layers, and finally an output layer.
architecture_details.resnet50_details: Deep neural network with 50 layers, using shortcuts/skip connections to address vanishing gradients. Consists of convolutional layers, identity blocks, convolution blocks, and fully connected layers.
architecture_details.vgg19_details: Deep convolutional neural network with 19 layers, developed by Visual Geometry Group. Characterized by 3x3 convolution layers followed by max pooling and fully connected layers.
architecture_details.densenet121_details: Deep neural network with dense feature connectivity pattern where each layer is interconnected to every other layer in a feed-forward way. Includes bottleneck and compression layers.
architecture_details.unfrozen_layers: First 10 layers of VGG19 and last 15 layers of ResNet50 were unfrozen and set as trainable.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (hyperparameter tuning mentioned but specific optimizer like Adam/SGD not named).
training_details.hyperparameters: Different hyperparameter tuning was applied to hybrid models (VGG19+ResNet50 and VGG19+DenseNet121). Specific values not reported.
training_details.training_schedule: Models trained separately then combined. Data augmentation applied during training.
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation, flipping, zooming. Target class balance of ~2000 images per class via augmentation.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Multi-class classification into 7 classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc).
classification_or_output_head.head_details: Final output layer after Dense1, Dropout, and Dense2 layers in the hybrid architecture.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Augmented Data Hybrid Model (VGG19+ResNet with Data Augmentation)
preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: rotation, flipping, and zooming to balance class distribution (targeting ~2000 images per class)
Class balancing via reduction of the majority class (NV) to 2000 images
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) to assign additional weights to classes and handle class imbalance
Mapping image names with lesion IDs and types and storing them in separate folders
80-20 split of the dataset into training (10,595 images) and validation (2,646 images) sets
Transfer learning with unfreezing of specific layers (first 10 layers of VGG19 and last 15 layers of ResNet50) to adapt pretrained weights

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to images in each class to balance the dataset distribution. Images were iteratively augmented until a target count of approximately 2000 images per class was reached.
The majority class (NV/Melanocytic nevi) was reduced from 6705 images to 2000 images to facilitate balanced augmentation of minority classes.
Used to assign additional weights to classes to handle class imbalance, specifically tested with DenseNet121 and ResNet50 models.

conclusions: The HAM10000 dataset is highly imbalanced, with class counts ranging from 92 (DF) to 6705 (NV), which negatively impacts model performance if not addressed.

Individual models exhibit varying strengths; for instance, ResNet50 achieved a 90% true vs. predicted label ratio for the akiec class, while VGG19 performed poorly on the imbalanced dataset even with SMOTE.
Initial hybrid models (VGG19+ResNet and VGG19+DenseNet) without data augmentation performed worse than individual models (72% and 74% accuracy respectively).
Hyperparameter tuning improved hybrid model accuracy (e.g., VGG19+DenseNet to 83%, VGG19+ResNet V3 to 86%), but classwise accuracy remained suboptimal.
Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming) to balance classes (targeting ~2000 images per class) was essential for handling class imbalance, as SMOTE alone was insufficient.
The Augmented Data Hybrid Model (VGG19+ResNet) achieved the best performance with an overall F1 score of 86-87% and classwise F1-scores ranging from 77% to 100%.
Hybrid architectures effectively combine the complementary strengths of different CNNs (e.g., ResNet50's residual connections and VGG19's robustness) for more accurate skin cancer classification.

Future work should explore more robust data augmentation techniques like GANs, classical image preprocessing, feature extraction, and training hybrid models from scratch to potentially improve results.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to be highly imbalanced (NV class has 6705 images vs. DF class with 92), potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or datasets.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset using an 80-20 split, raising concerns about overfitting or dataset-specific bias.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance differences between models, relying solely on accuracy and F1-scores.

Data leakage risk exists as the paper mentions taking 'at least 50 images OR 20% of images from original data to test directory' but does not clearly specify if this test set was held out during the augmentation and hyperparameter tuning phases.

The best-performing model (VGG19+ResNet with augmentation) achieved an overall F1 score of 86-87%, which may be considered modest for clinical deployment compared to state-of-the-art methods often exceeding 90% on this dataset.
Specific hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, loss function) are not explicitly reported, making reproducibility difficult.

The paper acknowledges that initial hybrid models performed worse than individual models without augmentation, highlighting the critical dependency on data balancing techniques like SMOTE and geometric augmentation.
The study is published in a conference proceedings (AISTS), which may have less rigorous peer review compared to top-tier journals, and the sample size is limited to a single public dataset.
